title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models
abstract:
We explore how generating a chain of thought—a series of intermediate reasoning steps—significantly improves the ability of large language models to perform complex reasoning. In particular, we show how such reasoning abilities emerge naturally in sufficiently large language models through a simple method called chain-of-thought prompting, in which a few chain-of-thought demonstrations are provided as exemplars in the prompt.

Experiments on three large language models show that chain-of-thought prompting improves performance across a range of arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. The empirical gains can be striking. For instance, prompting PaLM 540B with just eight chain-of-thought exemplars achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems, surpassing even fine-tuned GPT-3 with a verifier.

Figure 1: Chain-of-thought prompting enables large language models to tackle complex arithmetic, commonsense, and symbolic reasoning tasks. Chain-of-thought reasoning processes are highlighted.

Figure 2: PaLM 540B uses chain-of-thought prompting to achieve new state-of-the-art performance on the GSM8K benchmark of math word problems. Fine-tuned GPT-3 and prior best results are from Cobbe et al. (2021).

title: s1: Simple Test-Time Scaling
abstract:
Test-time scaling is a promising new approach to language modeling that uses additional computation during inference to improve performance. Recently, OpenAI’s o1 model demonstrated this capability but did not publicly disclose its methodology, leading to numerous replication efforts.

This work explores the simplest approach to achieving test-time scaling and strong reasoning performance. First, the authors curate a small dataset, s1K, consisting of 1,000 questions paired with reasoning traces. The dataset is built using three validated criteria: difficulty, diversity, and quality.

Second, they introduce budget forcing, a technique for controlling test-time computation by either forcefully terminating the model’s reasoning process or extending it by appending the word "Wait" multiple times whenever the model attempts to finish. This encourages the model to reconsider its reasoning, often correcting earlier mistakes.

After supervised fine-tuning of the Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct language model on the s1K dataset and applying budget forcing, the resulting model, s1-32B, outperforms o1-preview on competition mathematics benchmarks by up to 27% on MATH and AIME24. Furthermore, increasing test-time computation through budget forcing improves performance beyond the model’s baseline, raising its AIME24 accuracy from 50% to 57%.

The authors have released the model, dataset, and source code as open source.

title: A Survey of Scaling in Large Language Model Reasoning
abstract:
Rapid advancements in large language models (LLMs) have significantly enhanced their reasoning capabilities, driven by various strategies such as multi-agent collaboration. However, unlike the well-established performance gains achieved by scaling data and model size, scaling reasoning in LLMs is more complex and can even degrade reasoning performance, introducing new challenges related to model alignment and robustness.

This survey provides a comprehensive examination of reasoning scaling in LLMs, categorizing it into multiple dimensions and analyzing how different scaling strategies contribute to improved reasoning capabilities.

The survey first explores scaling in input size, which enables LLMs to process and leverage larger contexts for more effective reasoning. It then examines scaling in reasoning steps, which enhances multi-step inference and logical consistency. Next, it discusses scaling in reasoning rounds, where iterative interactions progressively refine reasoning outcomes. The survey also covers training-enabled reasoning, focusing on iterative optimization techniques that improve model reasoning through training.

Finally, the authors highlight promising future research directions for advancing LLM reasoning. By synthesizing these diverse perspectives, the survey provides a unified understanding of how different scaling strategies fundamentally enhance the reasoning capabilities of LLMs and offers guidance for the development of next-generation AI systems.

title: MathScale: Scaling Instruction Tuning for Mathematical Reasoning
abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable problem-solving capabilities, yet their performance on mathematical reasoning tasks remains limited. To address this issue, the authors propose MathScale, a simple and scalable framework for generating high-quality mathematical reasoning data using frontier LLMs such as GPT-3.5.

Inspired by the cognitive process of human mathematical learning, MathScale first extracts topics and knowledge points from a set of seed math questions and constructs a concept graph. This graph is then used to generate new mathematical problems, enabling scalable dataset expansion. The approach demonstrates effective scalability with respect to the size of the generated mathematics dataset.

Using this framework, the authors create MathScaleQA, a large-scale mathematical reasoning dataset containing two million question-answer pairs. To comprehensively evaluate mathematical reasoning, they also introduce MWPBENCH, a benchmark composed of ten math word problem datasets, including GSM8K and MATH, spanning K-12, college-level, and competition-level mathematics.

Fine-tuning open-source LLMs, such as LLaMA-2 and Mistral, on MathScaleQA significantly improves their mathematical reasoning capabilities. On MWPBENCH, MathScale-7B achieves state-of-the-art performance across all evaluated datasets, outperforming the strongest models of comparable size by 42.9% in micro-average accuracy and 43.7% in macro-average accuracy.

title: Scaling Up Test-Time Compute with Latent Reasoning: A Recurrent Depth Approach
abstract:
We study a novel language model architecture capable of scaling test-time computation by implicitly reasoning in latent space. Our model works by iterating a recurrent block, thereby unrolling to arbitrary depth at test time. This stands in contrast to mainstream reasoning models that scale computation by producing more tokens.

Unlike approaches based on chain-of-thought reasoning, our approach does not require specialized training data, can work with small context windows, and can capture types of reasoning that are not easily represented in words. We scale a proof-of-concept model to 3.5 billion parameters and 800 billion tokens. We show that the resulting model can improve its performance on reasoning benchmarks, sometimes dramatically, up to a computational load equivalent to 50 billion parameters.

title: rStar-Math: Small LLMs Can Master Math Reasoning with Self-Evolved Deep Thinking
abstract:
We present rStar-Math to demonstrate that small language models (SLMs) can rival or even surpass the mathematical reasoning capabilities of OpenAI o1 without distillation from superior models. rStar-Math achieves this by exercising "deep thinking" through Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS), where a math policy SLM performs test-time search guided by an SLM-based process reward model.

rStar-Math introduces three innovations to address the challenges of training the two SLMs: (1) a novel code-augmented chain-of-thought data synthesis method that performs extensive MCTS rollouts to generate step-by-step verified reasoning trajectories used to train the policy SLM; (2) a novel process reward model training method that avoids naïve step-level score annotation, yielding a more effective process preference model (PPM); and (3) a self-evolution recipe in which the policy SLM and PPM are built from scratch and iteratively evolved to improve reasoning capabilities.

Through four rounds of self-evolution with millions of synthesized solutions for 747,000 math problems, rStar-Math boosts SLMs’ mathematical reasoning to state-of-the-art levels. On the MATH benchmark, it improves Qwen2.5-Math-7B from 58.8% to 90.0% and Phi-3 Mini 3.8B from 41.4% to 86.4%, surpassing o1-preview by 4.5 and 0.9 percentage points, respectively. On the American Invitational Mathematics Examination (AIME), rStar-Math solves an average of 53.3% of the problems, equivalent to 8 out of 15.
